parsed_findings.chain_steps: Send a path with repeated traversal segments to bypass the one-shot replace and hit files outside the intended directory.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can read arbitrary files reachable from the process user.
parsed_findings.proof: `replace('../','')` turns `../../etc/passwd` into `../etc/passwd`, and `path.join('/srv/data', ...)` resolves outside the base dir.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `/download?file=../../etc/passwd` and read a file above `/srv/data`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Only the first `../` is stripped, so `../../etc/passwd` still escapes `/srv/data` after `path.join`.

results.raw_output: {"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-22","file":"files.js","line_start":2,"line_end":3,"vuln_claim":"Only the first `../` is stripped, so `../../etc/passwd` still escapes `/srv/data` after `path.join`.","proof":"`replace('../','')` turns `../../etc/passwd` into `../etc/passwd`, and `path.join('/srv/data', ...)` resolves outside the base dir.","repro_steps":"Request `/download?file=../../etc/passwd` and read a file above `/srv/data`.","impact":"An attacker can read arbitrary files reachable from the process user.","confidence":0.98,"chain_steps":"Send a path with repeated traversal segments to bypass the one-shot replace and hit files outside the intended directory."}]}

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Forge `state`, base64-encode a pickle gadget, and trigger `/resume` to execute it.
parsed_findings.impact: This can lead to remote code execution or full application compromise.
parsed_findings.proof: `pickle.loads(base64.b64decode(raw))` runs attacker-controlled pickle opcodes from `request.cookies['state']`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `/resume` with a crafted `state` cookie containing a malicious pickle payload.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted cookie data is deserialized with `pickle.loads`, enabling code execution if an attacker forges `state`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: If stored user fields include privilege flags, this becomes direct privilege escalation.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can alter other users' profiles, flags, or credentials.
parsed_findings.proof: The handler loads db.users.findById(req.params.id) and saves it without any ownership or authorization check on the target record.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: PATCH /api/users/<victim-id> as a normal user with any JSON body and observe the victim record change.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Any authenticated user can patch arbitrary accounts because the route never verifies that :id belongs to req.user or a privileged role.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Combine this with the unrestricted :id parameter to modify a chosen victim.
parsed_findings.impact: This can escalate privileges or hijack accounts.
parsed_findings.proof: Every attacker-controlled key in req.body is copied into the persisted user object before save.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send PATCH with fields like isAdmin:true or role:'admin' and see them persist.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Object.assign(user, req.body) lets attackers overwrite sensitive user attributes such as roles, flags, or password hashes.

parsed_findings.impact: Remote code execution as the app user.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Submit payload -> markdown renders unsafe HTML -> stored in DB -> returned by `/comments` for browser rendering.
parsed_findings.impact: A malicious comment can execute script in viewers' sessions and steal data or perform actions as them.
parsed_findings.proof: `markdown.markdown(body)` can emit raw HTML, and the rendered result is persisted in `html` without any filtering.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/comments` with a body like ``, then view the stored comment in a browser.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted comment body is rendered to HTML with no sanitization, so attacker-controlled markup can be stored and later executed as XSS.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Submit a negative amount to bypass the balance check, then use the resulting balance increase to repeat transfers.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can mint money from thin air and drain other users' balances.
parsed_findings.proof: `amt = int(...)` accepts `-1`, `src < amt` passes, then line 8 adds funds to the sender and line 9 subtracts from the recipient.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `{"to":"victim","amount":-1}` as an authenticated user with any balance.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `amount` is never constrained to a positive value, so a negative transfer credits the sender and debits the recipient.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Race two POSTs so each reads the same `src`, then both subtract and commit independently.
parsed_findings.impact: Concurrent requests can overdraw the account and corrupt balances.
parsed_findings.proof: Lines 5-9 do a read-check-write sequence with separate balance writes and no lock or transaction boundary.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Start two transfers at once for the same source balance and observe both pass line 6 before either write lands.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The balance check and two updates are not atomic, so concurrent transfers can race and spend the same funds twice.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Forge a JWT with admin claims, break the signature, and rely on the production fallback to accept the decoded payload.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can forge identity and claims, leading to full auth bypass and privilege escalation.
parsed_findings.proof: The catch block returns `jwt.decode(t)` whenever `NODE_ENV == 'production'`, so invalid or forged JWTs are treated as authenticated data.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a tampered JWT with a bad RS256 signature while `NODE_ENV=production`; `jwt.verify` throws, then the code falls back to `decode`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: In production, any token that fails verification is still accepted via `jwt.decode`, bypassing signature checks.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use crafted object values in both fields to turn the equality lookup into an operator-based match.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may bypass authentication and gain access to accounts without knowing valid credentials.
parsed_findings.proof: If `req.body.email` is an object like `{"$ne":null}`, `findOne(q)` can match unintended users instead of a literal email.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/login` with JSON body `{"email":{"$ne":null},"password":{"$ne":null}}` and observe the query can authenticate a user.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `email` and `password` are copied into a Mongo-style query, enabling NoSQL operator injection and login bypass.

parsed_findings.impact: Key disclosure breaks confidentiality for all past and future encrypted data using this utility.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 2 defines KEY as a fixed 16-byte literal instead of deriving or loading it securely.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Inspect the source and note that every call to enc() uses the same embedded key.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The AES key is hardcoded in source, so anyone who can read the code can decrypt all ciphertexts or forge encrypted data.

results.raw_output: {"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-321","file":"crypto_util.py","line_start":2,"line_end":2,"vuln_claim":"The AES key is hardcoded in source, so anyone who can read the code can decrypt all ciphertexts or forge encrypted data.","proof":"Line 2 defines KEY as a fixed 16-byte literal instead of deriving or loading it securely.","repro_steps":"Inspect the source and note that every call to enc() uses the same embedded key.","impact":"Key disclosure breaks confidentiality for all past and future encrypted data using this utility.","confidence":0.98,"chain_steps":""}]}

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Invoke the function with a NULL destination to force a crash.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can trigger a denial of service by supplying a NULL output buffer.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 3 returns only when `out` is non-NULL, then line 5 writes to `out` without validation.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `copy_name("x", NULL, 8)` and it will crash at `memcpy`/`out[n]`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The null check is inverted, so a NULL `out` pointer reaches `memcpy` and dereferences NULL.

results.raw_output: {"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-476","file":"safe_copy.c","line_start":3,"line_end":5,"vuln_claim":"The null check is inverted, so a NULL `out` pointer reaches `memcpy` and dereferences NULL.","proof":"Line 3 returns only when `out` is non-NULL, then line 5 writes to `out` without validation.","repro_steps":"Call `copy_name(\"x\", NULL, 8)` and it will crash at `memcpy`/`out[n]`.","impact":"An attacker can trigger a denial of service by supplying a NULL output buffer.","confidence":0.98,"chain_steps":"Invoke the function with a NULL destination to force a crash."}]}
